concise:
Ayah 1–2 · linked video: 01. Al-Qalam (Ayah 1-2)

Surah Al-Qalam: Ayahs 1-2

Ayah 1

نٓ ۚ وَٱلْقَلَمِ وَمَا يَسْطُرُونَ

Noon. By the pen and what they write.

Allah ﷻ begins this surah with a divine oath, swearing by the pen and whatever is continuously written by it. The letter “noon” stands as one of the mysterious letters that opens certain surahs of the Quran, inviting deeper contemplation of the divine message that follows.

The oath itself is remarkable in its simplicity yet profound in its implications. Allah ﷻ swears by two things: the pen as an instrument, and whatever is being recorded through its use. In Arabic grammar, when a pronoun appears without a clear antecedent, it creates an intentional ambiguity that opens multiple layers of meaning. The word “they” in “what they write” remains deliberately unspecified, allowing for various interpretations that enrich our understanding.

The Identity of “They Who Write”

Several scholarly interpretations exist regarding who “they” refers to in this oath. One perspective identifies them as the angels who record our deeds, positioned on either shoulder, documenting every action we take. Another understanding points to the angels who wrote down revelation from the Preserved Tablet (Al-Lawh Al-Mahfuz), bringing it to the messengers to teach them what Allah ﷻ had revealed.

A third interpretation connects this to the scribes of previous revelations—the scholars and writers who transmitted the Torah and the Gospel. Unlike the Islamic tradition of memorization, the People of the Book preserved their scriptures primarily through written documentation, establishing systems of scribes who carefully recorded divine revelation.

The Pen as Proof

The significance of this oath becomes clearer when we understand its purpose. Allah ﷻ uses the pen and what is written as proof for what follows: the assertion that the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ is not insane. The centuries of Jewish and Christian scholarship that existed before the Prophet’s ﷺ mission contain enough evidence within them to confirm that what he brought was truth, not madness. Their own moving pens, their own documented traditions, became witnesses to his prophethood.

The verb form used here (يَسْطُرُونَ - “they write”) encompasses both past and ongoing action. It refers not only to what had already been written by the time of revelation, but also to what would continue to be written. Consider the vast ocean of Islamic scholarship that would emerge from this revelation: books of Tafsir, Hadith, Fiqh, Aqeedah, and countless other sciences. The sheer volume and depth of knowledge that would be produced as a result of this message stands as evidence that the one who brought it could not have been insane.

The Impact on Knowledge

The Quran’s influence on human knowledge and the philosophy of learning itself would become undeniable proof of the Prophet’s ﷺ truthfulness. Time would tell that he was not insane at all. Future generations would witness how this revelation shaped civilizations, elevated the pursuit of knowledge, and established frameworks for understanding that would benefit humanity across centuries.

Historical Context

According to some scholars, the opening ayahs of this surah may have been among the earliest revelations, possibly the second revelation after the first ayahs of Surah Al-Alaq (اقْرَأْ بِاسْمِ رَبِّكَ الَّذِي خَلَقَ - “Read in the name of your Lord who created”). That first revelation also contained a reference to the pen: “Who taught by the pen” (عَلَّمَ بِالْقَلَمِ). When the Prophet ﷺ returned from that overwhelming experience and began to share what had occurred, some among those close to him wondered if perhaps he had lost his mind. In that moment of vulnerability and uncertainty, Allah ﷻ sent this confirmation.

Ayah 2

مَآ أَنتَ بِنِعْمَةِ رَبِّكَ بِمَجْنُونٍ

You are not, by the favor of your Lord, insane at all.

This ayah provides the answer to the oath that preceded it. The double negative construction in Arabic (مَآ…بِ) creates an emphatic negation—“not at all.” Allah ﷻ is not merely denying the accusation; He is emphatically rejecting it with absolute certainty.

The Favor of Your Lord

Notice the phrase “by the favor of your Lord” (بِنِعْمَةِ رَبِّكَ) positioned within this declaration. Allah ﷻ could have simply said “You are not insane,” but He chose to embed within this statement a reminder of His special favor upon the Prophet ﷺ. This favor encompasses the gift of prophethood itself, the revelation of the Quran, and the unique position the Prophet ﷺ holds in relation to his Lord.

The use of “your Lord” (رَبِّكَ) rather than a more general reference to Allah ﷻ creates an intimate, personal connection. It emphasizes the special relationship between the Prophet ﷺ and his Creator, the One who raised him, nurtured him, and prepared him for this monumental responsibility.

The Challenge of Believing in Oneself

This reassurance was crucial not only as a response to the accusations of others, but as a support for the Prophet ﷺ himself. We often forget that while we as believers must have faith in the Prophet ﷺ, he himself had to have faith in his own prophethood. This is a dimension of Iman that is rarely discussed—the Prophet ﷺ had to believe in himself as a messenger of Allah ﷻ.

Imagine the psychological and spiritual weight of that reality. When you are the only one proclaiming something so extraordinary, when those closest to you begin to doubt your sanity, when the entire foundation of your society ridicules your message—in such circumstances, maintaining conviction in your own truthfulness requires tremendous strength. The Prophet ﷺ needed divine confirmation, not just for the sake of responding to critics, but for his own heart’s reassurance in the face of such a traumatic and overwhelming experience.

A Message of Perseverance

This ayah carries within it a profound lesson about perseverance in the face of doubt and accusations. When people question your sanity for holding firm to truth, when they mock you for your convictions, when even those you love begin to wonder if you’ve lost your way—remember that truth doesn’t become falsehood because people reject it. The Prophet ﷺ was given this divine reassurance at a moment when he needed it most, and through it, we too receive strength to stand firm when our faith is questioned or ridiculed.

Ayah 2–15 · linked video: 02. Al-Qalam (Ayah 2-15)

Surah Al-Qalam: Ayahs 2-15

Ayah 2

مَآ أَنتَ بِنِعْمَةِ رَبِّكَ بِمَجْنُونٍ

In the context of Makkan society, when Allah ﷻ declares, “You are not, by the favor of your Lord, insane,” we must understand the weight of this statement. Imagine being told by everyone around you that you’ve lost your mind. Even your beloved wife, who supports you out of love, questions what’s happening. The elders you’ve respected your entire life, the wise leaders of your community, all of them are calling you crazy. In such circumstances, you might be willing to give their doubts an ear.

This is why at the end of Surah Al-Baqarah, Allah ﷻ says: آمَنَ الرَّسُولُ بِمَآ أُنزِلَ إِلَيْهِ مِن رَّبِّهِۦ وَٱلْمُؤْمِنُونَ - “The Messenger has believed in what was revealed to him from his Lord, and so have the believers.” The Prophet ﷺ himself had to take a leap of faith. He too had to believe in the face of overwhelming opposition.

Ayah 3

وَإِنَّ لَكَ لَأَجْرًا غَيْرَ مَمْنُونٍ

“And indeed, for you is a reward uninterrupted.” The word مَمْنُونٍ comes from the root that means to cut or diminish. Allah ﷻ is assuring His Messenger ﷺ that his compensation will never stop, never be reduced, never be interrupted.

This continuity manifests in remarkable ways. The Prophet ﷺ was given al-Maqam al-Mahmud, the Praised Station, an honor beyond our comprehension. But beyond that, every time someone says “peace and blessings be upon him,” his reward increases. Every time someone offers praise, his rank elevates. Every single remembrance of Allah ﷻ that you and I make as Muslims, he has a share in it because he was our teacher. Every good deed performed by any Muslim who has ever lived over the past millennium and a half, the Prophet ﷺ has a commission in that deed.

His rank continues to rise, continuously, perpetually. And we get to contribute to that elevation - that is our honor. We get to contribute to the honor of our Messenger ﷺ.

Ayah 4

وَإِنَّكَ لَعَلَىٰ خُلُقٍ عَظِيمٍ

“And indeed, you are of a tremendous character.” When we connect this ayah back to the opening oath - the pen and what it records - we see multiple layers of proof for the Prophet’s ﷺ truthfulness. The very fact that what the angels transcribe comes from the sacred preserved tablet (اللوح المحفوظ) and is delivered through Jibreel (AS) to the Prophet ﷺ is itself evidence that this is not the product of insanity or jinn possession. The source itself testifies to the authenticity of the message.

But then Allah ﷻ adds another proof: the exceptional character of His Messenger ﷺ. This is crucial to understand - Allah ﷻ is praising the morals and personality of the Prophet ﷺ even before revelation came to him. His character as a human being, his dealings with people, his trustworthiness, his integrity - these qualities that he possessed before prophethood are being used as validation of his truthfulness.

The word خُلُق refers specifically to how you deal with الخَلْق (creation). Your dealings with Allah’s ﷻ creation make up your character. The Prophet’s ﷺ great khuluq is what protected him from accusations of insanity and what qualified him for the mission he was given.

Here we must pause and reflect on ourselves as Muslims. We love the Book, we recite it, we memorize it, we teach it, we discuss it. But what’s missing from underneath is the character of Muslims. Our business dealings, our political interactions, our social conduct, our economic transactions - all of them are lacking in moral character. And you don’t need advanced degrees in Islamic jurisprudence to have morality. You just need to have a conscience, a human conscience. Yet this has been stripped from much of the Muslim community.

Look at the statistics of the most corrupt nations, the nations downloading the most harmful content, the nations failing at every measurable level of ethical conduct. Where is the khuluq? Where is the character?

This reality becomes painfully visible even in the holiest places. You see beautiful recitation of the Quran and magnificent mosques with marble courtyards, yet two streets away, people don’t know how to speak respectfully to each other. People don’t understand what it means to stand in an orderly line - and we are the religion of lines, the religion of organization and discipline. The closer you get to the House of Allah ﷻ, the more some people seem to lose their Islam. The closer you approach the Haram, the more elbows get thrown.

You can be looking at Jabal al-Nur, the mountain where revelation first came, while standing in a pile of trash - not in a garbage area, but in the street - because some Muslims don’t care where they throw their refuse. Where is the khuluq? Where is the character?

You walk toward the masjid and pass twenty children either trying to sell you calendars or begging because they’re physically deformed. This is the capital of Islam. Something is fundamentally wrong, and we’re not thinking deeply enough about it.

This ayah also addresses a modern controversy, though calling it a controversy gives it more weight than it deserves. Some people question whether we really need to follow the Sunnah - is it truly that important? Those who don’t want to say this directly will ask in roundabout ways: “Isn’t the Quran enough?” This is their method of saying, “Don’t bother me with the Sunnah.”

But Allah ﷻ didn’t just validate the Quran itself when He revealed these ayahs. He validated the character of His Messenger ﷺ even before the Quran came. If you believe in the Quran, then you must believe that whatever Allah ﷻ makes a big deal about should be a big deal to you. The character of the Prophet ﷺ, his conduct, his mannerisms, his life - these are a big deal because Allah ﷻ made them a big deal.

Ayah 5-6

فَسَتُبْصِرُ وَيُبْصِرُونَ بِأَييِّكُمُ ٱلْمَفْتُونُ

“So you will see, and they will see, which of you is the afflicted one.” The word مَفْتُون comes from فِتْنَة, and in this context refers to someone who has lost their mind because of traumatic incidents or deeply disturbing events. Allah ﷻ is saying: Soon enough, the truth will become clear. Soon you’ll discover who really stands where, and who has truly lost their senses.

Ayah 7

إِنَّ رَبَّكَ هُوَ أَعْلَمُ بِمَن ضَلَّ عَن سَبِيلِهِۦ وَهُوَ أَعْلَمُ بِٱلْمُهْتَدِينَ

“Indeed, your Lord is most knowing of who has strayed from His way, and He is most knowing of the rightly guided.” No doubt, your Master knows better who has gone astray from His path, and He is fully aware of those who are committed to guidance.

Ayah 8

فَلَا تُطِعِ ٱلْمُكَذِّبِينَ

“So do not obey the deniers.” Don’t give in to those who try to nullify your message by calling it a lie, who call you a liar. Don’t take influence from them, don’t let their pressure affect your stance.

Ayah 9

وَدُّوا۟ لَوْ تُدْهِنُ فَيُدْهِنُونَ

“They wish that you would soften, so they would soften.” The word تُدْهِن comes from دُهْن, which means oil. When you take hard vegetables and cook them in oil until they soften, this process is called إِدْهان. The concept here is negotiation, compromise - when you talk to someone trying to soften their position: “You give a little, we’ll give a little.”

This is exactly what the enemies of Islam wanted. The stories are well known from the Seerah: they came to the Prophet ﷺ asking, “What do you want? Women? We can arrange that. Do you want to be our tribal leader? Go ahead, we’ll make you our chief. Money? We’ll give you piles of wealth - just stop this preaching. Or perhaps we can work out a schedule: during the day we worship your God, at night we do our thing. Let’s just reach some kind of arrangement. After all, we’re from the same tribe, right? We need to stick together.”

Allah ﷻ reveals their true desire: وَدُّوا۟ لَوْ تُدْهِنُ - “They really want you to soften your stance just a bit.” They want you to compromise, to negotiate on principles. “We’ll stop calling you insane - we know that hurts, and we’re sorry it came to that - but if you just work with us a little here, we can all reach a happy resolution.” That’s what they wanted.

And you’ll see by the end of the next surah what Allah ﷻ declares if the Prophet ﷺ were to even consider such compromise. The language is among the strongest in the entire Quran directed toward the Prophet ﷺ - so strong that it’s difficult to translate adequately. When you read those words, you understand the gravity of even thinking about compromising the principles of this religion. But we’ll examine that passage when we reach it.

Another meaning of إِدْهان is to speak softly to someone even though you dislike them - giving them the impression that you’re their friend when you’re not, just to deceive them and play politics.

Ayah 10

وَلَا تُطِعْ كُلَّ حَلَّافٍ مَّهِينٍ

“And do not obey every worthless habitual swearer.” Don’t follow anyone who constantly takes oaths. This is the person who, while negotiating, keeps swearing: “I promise you, we’re one people. I mean well for you, I’m looking out for your interests. I swear to it, I swear!” The person who swears over and over again - this is حَلَّاف.

The word مَهِين is particularly insulting in Arabic. Some scholars say it refers specifically to Al-Walid ibn al-Mughirah, while others maintain it’s general language that could apply to anyone. Al-Walid was wealthy, held high status, and was known as a great debater among the Quraysh. He also had very strong sons, including Khalid ibn al-Walid (RA) (who would later embrace Islam and become one of Islam’s greatest generals). In Arabian society, your wealth meant something, but having strong sons elevated your status even further because sons were considered a valuable commodity.

By calling such a person mahin, Allah ﷻ uses a term that has multiple devastating meanings. Mahin is used for male camels that cannot impregnate the female - they’re not truly strong males. It’s a direct insult in Arabic culture. Mahin also refers to someone whose opinion carries little weight, someone lacking substance in what they say. Two key meanings emerge: little and weak. So Allah ﷻ is saying this person’s arguments are weak, his stance is weak, his positions lack substance. He may enjoy social status and popularity, but if you examine him for actual substance, there’s nothing there.

Ayah 11

هَمَّازٍ مَّشَّآءٍۭ بِنَمِيمٍ

“A slanderer, going about with malicious gossip.” The word هَمَّاز describes someone who is repeatedly condescending toward others, constantly putting people down. This is an intensive form - not just occasional but habitual behavior.

مَّشَّآءٍۭ بِنَمِيمٍ - someone who walks around spreading نَمِيمَة, which means speaking about people in ways that would hurt them or make them look bad, saying offensive things about others behind their backs. This person goes around insulting people, putting them down, and speaking negatively about them.

Notice that hammaz and masha are both intensive forms (صيغة مبالغة), indicating constant, repeated action.

Ayah 12

مَّنَّاعٍ لِّلْخَيْرِ مُعْتَدٍ أَثِيمٍ

“Preventer of good, transgressor, sinful.” مَّنَّاعٍ - another intensive form - describes someone who continuously prevents others from doing good. مُعْتَدٍ refers to one who constantly transgresses limits and boundaries.

أَثِيمٍ is a particularly powerful word. While آثِم means “one who sins,” أَثِيم indicates someone constantly immersed in sin - it suggests permanence and constancy. Consider the difference: someone might be jealous occasionally, but جَالِس means someone sitting, while جَلِيس means someone with a permanent seat, a reserved position. Athim means this person has a permanent, constant relationship with sin.

Why emphasize this constancy? Look at his qualities: he constantly swears, spreads his lowly opinions, speaks down to people, spreads rumors, says harmful things about others, and prevents people from doing good. Is his evil limited to himself? No - it spreads to others. So even when he’s sleeping, the consequences of his evil continue spreading. He’s earning what we might call in modern business terms “passive income” - except it’s passively earning sin. Even when he’s not actively sinning, the effects of his corruption continue to spread. This is what makes him athim.

Ayah 13

عُتُلٍّۭ بَعْدَ ذَٰلِكَ زَنِيمٍ

“Cruel, moreover, an illegitimate pretender.” عُتُلّ is another rare intensive form. ’Utull actually means to be harsh, rigid, tough - someone you don’t want to interact with. You know how some people have bosses who are mean and nasty? Usually they’re not in the office, but when they are, you think, “Oh God, I hope they don’t come to my desk. I hope we don’t have to talk because hurtful, cruel things come out of their mouth and they’re just not the kind of person you want to be around.”

There are people like this in every context - in workplaces, in schools, sometimes teachers, bosses, even older siblings. Just mean people. ’Utull is used for a prison guard who grabs a prisoner by the head and drags him into his cell with no regard for dignity. This person has no courtesy for anyone, doesn’t care about anybody, doesn’t care whose feelings he hurts. Extremely unlikable.

زَنِيمٍ is a play on words with multiple meanings. It could mean “humiliated.” But zanim also comes from a root related to a marked camel - in Arabian culture, they would cut the ear of certain camels to mark them as graceful, sacred animals that shouldn’t be slaughtered. By calling someone zanim, Allah ﷻ could be saying this person thinks he’s so graceful, so untouchable - like that protected camel - that nobody can criticize him, nobody can touch him. He considers himself above accountability.

Another interpretation: just as that camel is branded by having its ear cut, this person is branded - but by evil itself. That’s his true mark.

Ayah 14

أَن كَانَ ذَا مَالٍ وَبَنِينَ

“Because he possesses wealth and children.” And he thinks he’s so untouchable, so above everyone else - why? Simply because he has money and sons. Among Al-Walid’s sons was Khalid ibn al-Walid (RA), who would later become one of Islam’s greatest military commanders after accepting Islam.

Ayah 15

إِذَا تُتْلَىٰ عَلَيْهِ ءَايَـٰتُنَا قَالَ أَسَـٰطِيرُ ٱلْأَوَّلِينَ

“When Our verses are recited to him, he says, ‘Legends of the former peoples.’” أَسَاطِير means legends or myths. The word أُسْطُورَة comes from the same root and is related to the Greek word “historia,” which gives us the English word “story.” When Our ayahs are continuously recited to him over and over again, his dismissive response is always the same: “These are just ancient tales, legends of old civilizations.”

Ayah 15–42 · linked video: 03. Al-Qalam (Ayah 15-42)

Surah Al-Qalam: Ayahs 15-42

Ayah 15

إِذَا تُتْلَىٰ عَلَيْهِ ءَايَـٰتُنَا قَالَ أَسَـٰطِيرُ ٱلْأَوَّلِينَ

When Our ayahs are recited to him, he dismisses them as nothing more than أَسَـٰطِيرُ ٱلْأَوَّلِينَ - legends of the ancients. This rejection represents the height of arrogance, claiming that the divine revelation is merely old tales and folklore with no relevance or truth.

Ayah 16

سَنَسِمُهُۥ عَلَى ٱلْخُرْطُومِ

Allah ﷻ declares: We will brand him on the خُرْطُوم. The word khurtum refers to the trunk of an elephant, and it was also used to describe people with unusually large noses. However, the deeper figurative meaning in Arabic literature is profound. The expression “khurtumo al-qawm” refers to the most arrogant people in a nation - they are called “the noses” or “the trunks” of the people. Why? Because they walk around with their noses held high in pride and arrogance. They won’t lower their heads in humility, but instead keep their noses pointed upward, full of themselves.

When Allah ﷻ says He will brand him on his nose, He is saying He will mark his pride itself. The nose, held high in arrogance, will bear the permanent mark of his rejection and haughtiness.

Ayah 17

إِنَّا بَلَوْنَـٰهُمْ كَمَا بَلَوْنَآ أَصْحَـٰبَ ٱلْجَنَّةِ إِذْ أَقْسَمُوا۟ لَيَصْرِمُنَّهَا مُصْبِحِينَ

We have tested them just as We tested the people of the garden. This is the second story of garden owners in the Quran - the first was mentioned in Surah Al-Kahf. While both stories share similar themes, this one is more intense, though both conclude with important lessons.

These garden owners made an oath: they swore that when morning came - specifically مُصْبِحِينَ, at the earliest hours of dawn - they would harvest everything. The word صَرْم comes from the root related to sword or machete, referring specifically to the blunt cutting tools used to harvest fruit, branches and crops from farmland. They planned to cut down everything quickly, at the crack of dawn.

Ayah 18

وَلَا يَسْتَثْنُونَ

And they made no exceptions. They weren’t going to leave anything behind - no portion for the poor, no charity, nothing. Their plan was to harvest everything completely, leaving nothing for those in need.

Ayah 19

فَطَافَ عَلَيْهَا طَآئِفٌ مِّن رَّبِّكَ وَهُمْ نَآئِمُونَ

Then during the night before their planned harvest, a طَائِف - something that circles or swirls down - came from your Master while they were all sleeping. The word suggests movement that circles around, like the tawaf around the Kaaba. This could have been a tornado spinning down, or it could have been angels descending in a swirling motion to execute Allah’s decree. Whatever form it took, this visitor came from their Master while they were completely unaware, lost in sleep.

Ayah 20

فَأَصْبَحَتْ كَٱلصَّرِيمِ

By morning, their garden had become كَٱلصَّرِيمِ - as if already completely harvested and destroyed. The word sareem is powerful in its implications. When you harvest a garden normally, you might pluck the fruit or cut branches from the top. But sareem means something far more devastating - it describes when plants are completely severed from their roots. The entire garden was uprooted. Every tree, every plant, everything was rendered utterly useless. They couldn’t even use it for next year’s planting. Trees that take decades to grow were gone in a single night. All their investment had disappeared.

Ayah 21

فَتَنَادَوْا۟ مُصْبِحِينَ

They began calling out to each other in the morning. The word تَنَادَوْا means to call to one another. They woke up and started urgently calling: “Come on! It’s time to go! Let’s move!” But notice - they’re only calling each other مُصْبِحِينَ, in the morning, which implies they actually woke up late. They had sworn to go at the earliest dawn, but they’re only waking each other up later in the morning.

Ayah 22

أَنِ ٱغْدُوا۟ عَلَىٰ حَرْثِكُمْ إِن كُنتُمْ صَـٰرِمِينَ

“Let’s go to your crop in the later morning if you’re going to get this done!” The word غَدْوَة refers to the later part of the morning - the third hour, after subh (early dawn) and ishraq (sunrise). At least let’s get there before noon, they urged each other. They knew that in the afternoon, beggars and poor people typically show up. If those in need saw them heading to the farm with their cutting tools, they would realize it was harvest season and follow along, hoping for some charity. So they wanted to move quickly and quietly, before anyone could notice.

Ayah 23

فَٱنطَلَقُوا۟ وَهُمْ يَتَخَـٰفَتُونَ

They marched forward while يَتَخَـٰفَتُونَ - whispering to each other, talking very quietly. They were hiding their swords and cutting tools, speaking in hushed tones: “I can’t wait! Did you see the fruit yesterday? It was amazing!” “Shh! The neighbors might hear you!” They were afraid the poor people in the village might catch wind of what they were doing.

This reveals a disturbing mindset: they wanted to see and enjoy their wealth, but they didn’t want to see it while poor people were around. They wanted to keep everything to themselves. They didn’t even want to give poor people the idea that they might receive something, because that might make them feel obligated to actually give. Better to just keep it hidden entirely.

This attitude stands in stark contrast to what Allah ﷻ teaches in the Quran. Even in inheritance law, when relatives and others show up during the division of wealth, Allah ﷻ instructs: فَارْزُقُوهُم مِّنْهُ وَقُولُوا لَهُمْ قَوْلًا مَّعْرُوفًا - give them something from it and speak to them kind words. Don’t just turn them away. And immediately after, Allah ﷻ reminds those inheriting: What if you died and your children were standing in that position, in need? How would you want them to be treated?

Ayah 24

أَن لَّا يَدْخُلَنَّهَا ٱلْيَوْمَ عَلَيْكُم مِّسْكِينٌ

Their whispered conversation revealed their true intentions: “Don’t let a single poor person approach you today. No beggars. Remember last time? Too many showed up. We don’t want any of them - not a single مِسْكِين.”

This was a strong, emphatic warning they gave each other. One would grab another’s arm: “Not this time. Not one.” They didn’t want any connection with poor people, because if one showed up, he might bring his friends. If one person gave, others might feel social pressure to give as well. So their strategy was: nobody gives to anybody. They reinforced this pact among themselves.

Ayah 25

وَغَدَوْا۟ عَلَىٰ حَرْدٍ قَـٰدِرِينَ

They marched forward later in the morning عَلَىٰ حَرْدٍ - with angry, aggressive determination. The word hard describes someone moving forward with visible anger, looking like they’re about to confront someone. They were قَـٰدِرِينَ - completely in control, fully capable, marching with purpose.

Why this aggressive stance? Think about how beggars, street vendors, or children in need approach people. They look for someone with a kind face, someone smiling or approachable. They avoid people who look angry or aggressive, fearing they might be harmed or rejected harshly. These garden owners deliberately adopted this tough, angry appearance so that even if poor people saw them, they wouldn’t dare approach to ask for anything. They wanted to appear so intimidating that no one would even think about coming near them.

Ayah 26

فَلَمَّا رَأَوْهَا قَالُوٓا۟ إِنَّا لَضَآلُّونَ

Then when they finally arrived and saw their garden, their first reaction was shock and confusion: “We are absolutely lost! We took a wrong turn. This can’t be our farm. We must be in the wrong place.” The devastation was so complete that they couldn’t even recognize their own property.

Ayah 27

بَلْ نَحْنُ مَحْرُومُونَ

Then reality set in: “No - we have been deprived. We’ve been bankrupted.” The word مَحْرُومُونَ means something has been made haram for them - they’ve been blocked from accessing it, robbed of what they thought was theirs. The initial shock of thinking they were lost gave way to the devastating realization of what had actually happened.

Ayah 28

قَالَ أَوْسَطُهُمْ أَلَمْ أَقُل لَّكُمْ لَوْلَا تُسَبِّحُونَ

Then أَوْسَطُهُمْ - the most balanced one among them, the most level-headed - finally spoke up. He had clearly been overruled by the group’s peer pressure earlier, but now he said: “Didn’t I tell you? Why don’t you make tasbih? Why don’t you glorify Allah ﷻ?”

This reveals something crucial: this story isn’t just about a farm. This is about any wealth, any blessing that comes our way. Imagine you have a contract about to be finalized, a check coming in next week, a promotion, a bonus. You’re already celebrating, already planning where all that money will go. But the last thought on your mind is sadaqah, is gratitude to Allah ﷻ, is tasbih.

The first thought should be: a portion of this goes to those in need, as a demonstration of my gratitude to Allah ﷻ. That client you secured, that contract you signed, that promotion you received - these are all gifts from Allah ﷻ. As proof to yourself that you truly believe this comes from Him, a portion absolutely must go to charity. You should approach it spiritually: there’s no other way to look at this except that part of it belongs to those in need.

And as blessings come, you should increase in remembering Allah ﷻ, increase in tasbih. Why? Because money can become either a source of guidance or a source of misguidance. The money these farmers would earn through their garden - was that halal or haram? Completely halal. Earning money through legitimate farming is perfectly permissible. Earning a lot of money is not wrong in itself.

The problem wasn’t their earnings - it was their attitude. Not a single Islamic scholar would tell them they earned haram wealth. What scholars would identify is an ethical problem, a moral problem, a character problem. They didn’t want to give up any portion of their wealth, and they didn’t want to remember Allah ﷻ. These are spiritual issues, and sometimes Allah ﷻ teaches extremely hard lessons when people have spiritual problems. For these people, the only way they would learn was when all their wealth was taken away. Only then did they realize it had been coming from Allah ﷻ all along.

If you don’t want that to happen to you, realize it before the test comes. It’s all coming from Allah ﷻ. Have a portion designated for the poor. This is an important principle to instill in ourselves and in our children - whenever they receive something, a portion should be given away. Build this habit into their personality from young. If you’re giving your child money, don’t give them a single large bill. Give them smaller denominations so they can easily give one away. Make it easy for them to experience the joy of giving, and encourage this continuously.

We should do this ourselves, with gifts both big and small. Don’t miss an opportunity to give. Look for people in your actual social circle who need help. Yes, it’s good to support humanitarian causes - people starving in different parts of the world - and there are plenty of worthy causes. But diversify your sadaqah. Give to strangers through secure online donations, but also give to people you know personally. People who’ve lost their jobs, people struggling to pay rent. They’re not going to come and ask you directly. They won’t be on some website. The only way they’ll be helped is through you.

Allah ﷻ says تَعْرِفُهُم بِسِيمَاهُمْ - you’ll recognize them by their sima, the marks on their faces, the stress visible in their expressions. You don’t have to call it sadaqah. You can write a check and simply say “Eid Mubarak” or “This is for the kids” and give it quietly. Don’t embarrass them. Don’t say “This is fee sabeelillah because you’re from the poor.” Just be a decent person and help people out.

Especially in difficult economic times when people have lost jobs, when families are struggling to keep the lights on, when students can’t afford school - find out who’s having a hard time and help them. This isn’t just about Muslims. Your neighbors in your community, regardless of their faith, might be struggling. Help them out with no strings attached. Don’t make them read an Islamic book before you give them assistance. Just help because you should help, because you can help. That’s an act of virtue in itself, not necessarily tied to dawah or any other agenda.

Ayah 29

قَالُوا۟ سُبْحَـٰنَ رَبِّنَآ إِنَّا كُنَّا ظَـٰلِمِينَ

After this reminder, they responded: “SubhanAllah! Glory be to our Master! We were indeed the wrongdoers.” They finally acknowledged their transgression, their injustice against themselves and against those who deserved charity from their wealth.

Ayah 30

فَأَقْبَلَ بَعْضُهُمْ عَلَىٰ بَعْضٍ يَتَلَـٰوَمُونَ

Then they turned to each other, يَتَلَـٰوَمُونَ - blaming one another, sharing the blame back and forth. “It’s your fault! You’re the one who made us wake up late! You’re the one who convinced us not to give!” They went back and forth, each trying to shift responsibility onto the others.

Ayah 31

قَالُوا۟ يَـٰوَيْلَنَآ إِنَّا كُنَّا طَـٰغِينَ

Finally, they came to their senses and cried out: يَـٰوَيْلَنَآ! This expression is almost impossible to translate adequately into English. “Woe unto us” doesn’t even come close - it’s not in the same neighborhood of meaning. Ya waylana represents the kind of grief and anguish that ancient languages could express far more eloquently than modern ones. It’s the verbal equivalent of being speechless, of wailing, crying, screaming. The most unimaginable, horrific thing has happened. Nobody seriously says “woe unto us” when they’re genuinely grieving.

They continued: “We were indeed طَـٰغِينَ - rebellious, transgressing all bounds.”

Ayah 32

عَسَىٰ رَبُّنَآ أَن يُبْدِلَنَا خَيْرًا مِّنْهَآ إِنَّآ إِلَىٰ رَبِّنَا رَٰغِبُونَ

Yet even in their loss, they turned to hope: “Perhaps our Master will replace this garden with something better for us.” This is tawbah - genuine repentance. Because the garden had been completely uprooted, the only One who could restore it was Allah ﷻ. They finally recognized His power and said: “From here onwards, we’re not looking forward to our next harvest. We’re looking forward to Allah ﷻ. We are رَٰغِبُونَ - inclined, turned toward our Master.”

The word raghib is the opposite of ’anil in Arabic. Raghib means to be inclined toward someone, to turn to them. ’Anil means to be repelled from someone, to turn away from them and want nothing to do with them. Allah ﷻ uses ’anil when describing those who reject guidance: “Who would turn away from the legacy of Ibrahim (AS) except one who makes a fool of himself?” But these garden owners declared: إِلَىٰ رَبِّنَا رَٰغِبُونَ - “We will only be inclined toward our Master from now on.”

Ayah 33

كَذَٰلِكَ ٱلْعَذَابُ ۖ وَلَعَذَابُ ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ أَكْبَرُ ۚ لَوْ كَانُوا۟ يَعْلَمُونَ

This is what punishment is like. Allah ﷻ can send various forms of punishment in this world, and He mentioned different types in the previous surah - devastating winds, water sources being removed. In this surah, even gardens that seem secure can be eliminated overnight. These worldly punishments serve as warnings.

But then comes the crucial reminder: وَلَعَذَابُ ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ أَكْبَرُ - and the punishment of the Hereafter is far greater, far more severe. لَوْ كَانُوا۟ يَعْلَمُونَ - if only they knew, if only they had true knowledge of what awaits.

Ayah 34

إِنَّ لِلْمُتَّقِينَ عِندَ رَبِّهِمْ جَنَّـٰتِ ٱلنَّعِيمِ

For those who have taqwa, who are conscious of Allah ﷻ and mindful of His commands, there are جَنَّـٰتِ ٱلنَّعِيمِ in the company of their Master - gardens of permanent blessing, gardens of lasting luxury. The word na’eem is the opposite of sarim. Sarim represents complete destruction and depletion. Na’eem represents permanent, inexhaustible blessing. There will never be a downtime in these gardens, never a moment when the blessings cease or diminish.

Ayah 35

أَفَنَجْعَلُ ٱلْمُسْلِمِينَ كَٱلْمُجْرِمِينَ

Now Allah ﷻ poses a rhetorical question to those who deny the Hereafter: Will We make the Muslims like the criminals? Will We treat those who submit to Allah ﷻ the same as those who commit crimes against themselves and others?

There’s a philosophical claim in Western thought - even poets have expressed it - that “the earth is the final leveler.” The meaning is that all human beings, whether king or queen, CEO or janitor, rich or poor, end up equal under the earth when they die. Everyone’s grave looks roughly the same from above.

But Allah ﷻ categorically rejects this notion: No, you are not equal. It just appears that way on the surface. People will go under the earth, but the Muslims and the criminals will not be the same in the Hereafter.

Ayah 36

مَا لَكُمْ كَيْفَ تَحْكُمُونَ

مَا لَكُمْ - What’s wrong with you? كَيْفَ تَحْكُمُونَ - How do you make such judgments? How do you arrive at such conclusions? What kind of reasoning leads you to believe that the righteous and the wicked will have the same fate?

Ayah 37

أَمْ لَكُمْ كِتَـٰبٌ فِيهِ تَدْرُسُونَ

Or do you have some book in which you study these ideas? Do you have some divine revelation that tells you there’s no accountability, no difference between good and evil?

Ayah 38

إِنَّ لَكُمْ فِيهِ لَمَا تَخَيَّرُونَ

And in that imaginary book of yours, you can find whatever conclusions you want, whatever you choose to believe? In other words, you’ve created your own worldview based on your desires, not on any revealed truth. You’ve convinced yourself that you’re the owners of ultimate truth, that you can pick and choose your own version of reality and morality.

Ayah 39

أَمْ لَكُمْ أَيْمَـٰنٌ عَلَيْنَا بَـٰلِغَةٌ إِلَىٰ يَوْمِ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ ۙ إِنَّ لَكُمْ لَمَا تَحْكُمُونَ

Or do you have أَيْمَـٰنٌ - oaths, contracts, binding promises from Us? Do you have some guarantee from Allah ﷻ, some legal contract that extends بَـٰلِغَةٌ إِلَىٰ يَوْمِ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ - all the way until the Day of Judgment? Do you possess some kind of immunity, some assurance that you won’t be held accountable?

إِنَّ لَكُمْ لَمَا تَحْكُمُونَ - That whatever decisions and judgments you make in this life will automatically go in your favor in the next? Do you really believe you have such a guarantee?

Ayah 40

سَلْهُمْ أَيُّهُم بِذَٰلِكَ زَعِيمٌ

Ask them: which one of them is the زَعِيمٌ regarding this? Who among them has absolute proof, confident assumption, or guaranteed knowledge about their worldview? Who can stand up and say with certainty: “I have evidence that there’s no accountability, that everyone ends up the same”?

Ayah 41

أَمْ لَهُمْ شُرَكَآءُ فَلْيَأْتُوا۟ بِشُرَكَآئِهِمْ إِن كَانُوا۟ صَـٰدِقِينَ

Or do they have شُرَكَآءُ - partners, other beings who can help them, intervene for them on Judgment Day? If so, فَلْيَأْتُوا۟ بِشُرَكَآئِهِمْ - then let them bring forth these partners of theirs. Let them produce these supposed helpers, إِن كَانُوا۟ صَـٰدِقِينَ - if in fact they are telling the truth.

Ayah 42

يَوْمَ يُكْشَفُ عَن سَاقٍ وَيُدْعَوْنَ إِلَى ٱلسُّجُودِ فَلَا يَسْتَطِيعُونَ

The Day when the سَاق - the shin, the front part of the foot and ankle - will be exposed. This is one of the mutashabihat, the unclear or metaphorical verses of the Quran, describing scenes from the Day of Judgment in language that points to realities beyond our current comprehension.

On that Day, they will be called إِلَى ٱلسُّجُودِ - to prostrate - فَلَا يَسْتَطِيعُونَ - but they will not be able to. Those who refused to bow to Allah ﷻ in this life, who rejected His guidance and denied accountability, will find themselves unable to prostrate when it matters most. The opportunity for submission will have passed, and the reality they denied will surround them completely.

Ayah 42–52 · linked video: 04. Al-Qalam (Ayah 42-52)

PRIMARY_AYAH: 42

Ayah 42

يَوْمَ يُكْشَفُ عَن سَاقٍ وَيُدْعَوْنَ إِلَى ٱلسُّجُودِ فَلَا يَسْتَطِيعُونَ

The opening of this ayah presents us with a powerful image of the Day of Judgment through the phrase يَوْمَ يُكْشَفُ عَن سَاقٍ - “The Day when the shin is uncovered.” This expression carries deep meaning in the Arabic language, both literal and figurative.

In classical Arabic usage, when someone “shows their ankle” or shin, it indicates they are preparing for battle, rolling up their garments to march toward the enemy. The exposed ankle signals readiness for confrontation. This imagery conveys the gravity and intensity of that Day.

However, the scholars of Tafsir also explain that this phrase refers to Allah ﷻ manifesting His glory and power on the Day of Judgment in ways we cannot fully comprehend in this world. When Allah ﷻ reveals His majesty in this way, the natural human response would be to fall in prostration before such overwhelming glory.

Yet the ayah continues: وَيُدْعَوْنَ إِلَى ٱلسُّجُودِ فَلَا يَسْتَطِيعُونَ - “and they will be called to prostration, but they will not be able.” Despite being called to prostrate before the manifest glory of Allah ﷻ, these people will find themselves paralyzed, unable to make sujud. Allah ﷻ will withhold from them the very ability to bow down, even when His magnificence is undeniable before them.

Ayah 43

خَـٰشِعَةً أَبْصَـٰرُهُمْ تَرْهَقُهُمْ ذِلَّةٌ ۖ وَقَدْ كَانُوا۟ يُدْعَوْنَ إِلَى ٱلسُّجُودِ وَهُمْ سَـٰلِمُونَ

The description of these people on that Day continues: خَـٰشِعَةً أَبْصَـٰرُهُمْ - “their eyes humbled.” Terror will fill their vision. But more than that, تَرْهَقُهُمْ ذِلَّةٌ - “humiliation will cover them.” The word تَرْهَقُهُمْ suggests something that overwhelms and burdens, layer upon layer. These are backbreaking, crushing burdens of humiliation that stack upon them one after another. When someone is buried under so many problems that they cannot cope, the Arabs describe them as being consumed by those difficulties. This is the state of these disbelievers - buried under layers of disgrace.

The ayah then delivers a devastating contrast: وَقَدْ كَانُوا۟ يُدْعَوْنَ إِلَى ٱلسُّجُودِ وَهُمْ سَـٰلِمُونَ - “And they used to be called to prostration while they were sound.” The word سَـٰلِمُونَ emphasizes their complete capability. They were perfectly healthy, perfectly able, with no impairment whatsoever. They had every opportunity in this world to put down their pride and bow before their Creator, yet they refused.

Now on the Day of Judgment, they find themselves impaired and paralyzed, unable to do what they once had every ability to do. The tragedy is not in their current inability, but in their past refusal when they were fully capable.

Ayah 44

فَذَرْنِى وَمَن يُكَذِّبُ بِهَـٰذَا ٱلْحَدِيثِ ۖ سَنَسْتَدْرِجُهُم مِّنْ حَيْثُ لَا يَعْلَمُونَ

Allah ﷻ now addresses the situation directly: فَذَرْنِى وَمَن يُكَذِّبُ بِهَـٰذَا ٱلْحَدِيثِ - “So leave Me with whoever denies this statement.” The expression “leave Me” represents one of the most powerful manifestations of divine anger. It means: Let Me deal with him Myself. You need not concern yourself with their rejection - I will handle this matter personally.

The ayah continues with a chilling warning: سَنَسْتَدْرِجُهُم مِّنْ حَيْثُ لَا يَعْلَمُونَ - “We will progressively lead them from where they do not perceive.” The word اسْتِدْرَاج is a profound concept in Islamic understanding. It comes from the root daraja, which means to go up stairs or ranks. When someone ascends level by level, that is darajah, and when they descend level by level, that is nazool or going down.

Istidraj means to gradually elevate someone in a way that ultimately leads to their downfall. Allah ﷻ grants them more wealth, more power, more status, more influence - and with each increase in their worldly position, they commit more sins, more injustice, more evil. They think they are ascending, but they are actually being set up for a devastating fall. The higher the ladder you climb, the harder you crash when it is pulled from beneath you.

This is not a blessing but a warning - when someone persists in rejection and Allah ﷻ continues to grant them worldly success, it may be istidraj. They perceive it as divine approval, but it is actually a setup for ultimate accountability.

Ayah 45

وَأُمْلِى لَهُمْ ۚ إِنَّ كَيْدِى مَتِينٌ

Allah ﷻ continues: وَأُمْلِى لَهُمْ - “And I will give them time.” This respite, this of consequences, is deliberate. Allah ﷻ allows them to continue, to accumulate, to believe they are safe. The word إمْلَاء means to fill someone with hope and grant them extended time. It is both a mercy and a test - mercy because the door of repentance remains open, and a test because the extension can lead to increased heedlessness.

Then comes the definitive statement: إِنَّ كَيْدِى مَتِينٌ - “Indeed, My plan is firm.” The word مَتِينٌ refers to something unbreakable, like a rope that cannot be cut or the anchor of a ship that holds firm against any storm. Allah’s plan cannot be undone, cannot be escaped, cannot be broken. When He ﷻ decrees something, it is absolute and inevitable.

Ayah 46

أَمْ تَسْـَٔلُهُمْ أَجْرًا فَهُم مِّن مَّغْرَمٍ مُّثْقَلُونَ

Allah ﷻ now poses a rhetorical question to His Messenger ﷺ: أَمْ تَسْـَٔلُهُمْ أَجْرًا - “Or do you ask them for a payment?” The question challenges the disbelievers’ excuses for rejection. Are you demanding money from them in exchange for this message, like poets used to do when they would recite and then ask for spare change? In certain cities, you might see a street performer with a box in front of them where people throw coins. Is this what the Prophet ﷺ is doing?

The answer is obviously no, but Allah ﷻ completes the hypothetical: فَهُم مِّن مَّغْرَمٍ مُّثْقَلُونَ - “so they are weighed down by debt?” The word مَّغْرَمٍ refers to a penalty or fine, a burden of debt. Are they so heavily burdened by the cost of listening to you that they cannot bear it?

This exposes the absurdity of their rejection. The message comes free of charge, requiring no worldly payment, yet they treat it as though accepting it would bankrupt them. Their excuses are hollow.

Ayah 47

أَمْ عِندَهُمُ ٱلْغَيْبُ فَهُمْ يَكْتُبُونَ

Another rhetorical question follows: أَمْ عِندَهُمُ ٱلْغَيْبُ فَهُمْ يَكْتُبُونَ - “Or do they possess the unseen, so they write it down?” Do they have access to knowledge of the unseen realm? Can they record and decree what will happen in the future?

This question strikes at the heart of their arrogance. They reject the message about the Hereafter, about accountability, about resurrection - as if they have certain knowledge that it is false. But what knowledge do they possess? Have they witnessed the unseen? Can they write the future? Their rejection is based on nothing but assumption and pride.

Ayah 48

فَٱصْبِرْ لِحُكْمِ رَبِّكَ وَلَا تَكُن كَصَاحِبِ ٱلْحُوتِ إِذْ نَادَىٰ وَهُوَ مَكْظُومٌ

After addressing the disbelievers’ baseless rejection, Allah ﷻ returns to counsel His Messenger ﷺ: فَٱصْبِرْ لِحُكْمِ رَبِّكَ - “So be patient for the decree of your Lord.” Though the particle لِ is used here rather than حَتَّىٰ, it carries the meaning of “until” - remain patient until the verdict of your Master comes to pass. This is what scholars call لام التوقيت, the lam that indicates a time limit or deadline.

Then comes an important warning: وَلَا تَكُن كَصَاحِبِ ٱلْحُوتِ - “And do not be like the companion of the whale.” This refers to Prophet Yunus (AS), who is also called Dhun-Nun. The word نُون is one of the ancient Arabic terms for whale, and there is a beautiful consistency in this surah beginning with the letter Nun and ending with reference to the prophet associated with the whale.

The ayah describes the moment: إِذْ نَادَىٰ وَهُوَ مَكْظُومٌ - “when he called out while he was distressed.” The word مَكْظُومٌ describes someone completely overwhelmed, swallowed by their circumstances - which in Yunus’s (AS) case was literal, as he was inside the belly of the whale.

The comparison is not to criticize Prophet Yunus (AS), but to guide Prophet Muhammad ﷺ to a different path of patience. Where Yunus (AS) left his people in frustration, Muhammad ﷺ is being told to remain steadfast no matter how difficult the rejection becomes.

Ayah 49

لَّوْلَآ أَن تَدَٰرَكَهُۥ نِعْمَةٌ مِّن رَّبِّهِۦ لَنُبِذَ بِٱلْعَرَآءِ وَهُوَ مَذْمُومٌ

The ayah explains what happened to Yunus (AS): لَّوْلَآ أَن تَدَٰرَكَهُۥ نِعْمَةٌ مِّن رَّبِّهِۦ - “Had not a favor from his Lord reached him.” The word تَدَٰرَكَهُۥ means to come down to someone, to reach down and rescue them. Allah’s mercy descended upon him in the depths of the ocean, in the belly of the whale.

لَنُبِذَ بِٱلْعَرَآءِ وَهُوَ مَذْمُومٌ - “he would have been cast onto the naked shore while he was blamed.” The word عَرَآء refers to barren, empty land with no vegetation, no food, no water. The whale could have regurgitated him onto a deserted island where he would have perished. And in that state, وَهُوَ مَذْمُومٌ - he would have been blameworthy, still carrying the weight of having left his mission.

But Allah’s mercy intervened, changing the entire trajectory of events.

Ayah 50

فَٱجْتَبَـٰهُ رَبُّهُۥ فَجَعَلَهُۥ مِنَ ٱلصَّـٰلِحِينَ

Instead of that tragic end, فَٱجْتَبَـٰهُ رَبُّهُۥ - “So his Lord chose him.” The word اجْتَبَىٰ means to select someone because of their good qualities, to choose them for excellence. Despite his moment of leaving, despite his distress, Allah ﷻ saw the good in Yunus (AS) and chose him.

فَجَعَلَهُۥ مِنَ ٱلصَّـٰلِحِينَ - “and made him of the righteous.” This is the ultimate honor - to be counted among the righteous, to have your story end not in blame but in selection and elevation. The story of Yunus (AS) is one of redemption, of how Allah’s mercy can transform a moment of weakness into a manifestation of His grace.

Ayah 51

وَإِن يَكَادُ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ لَيُزْلِقُونَكَ بِأَبْصَـٰرِهِمْ لَمَّا سَمِعُوا۟ ٱلذِّكْرَ وَيَقُولُونَ إِنَّهُۥ لَمَجْنُونٌ

The surah now returns to the hostility faced by the Prophet ﷺ: وَإِن يَكَادُ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ لَيُزْلِقُونَكَ بِأَبْصَـٰرِهِمْ - “And indeed, those who disbelieve would almost make you slip with their eyes.” The word يُزْلِقُونَكَ captures a powerful image: to look at someone with such intensity, such intimidation, such hatred, that they become self-conscious and stumble or trip because of the weight of that stare.

This describes the hateful glares directed at the Prophet ﷺ. The disbelievers would fix their eyes on him with such hostility that the very look was meant to make him falter, to make him doubt, to make him fall. Just the way they looked at him was an act of aggression.

لَمَّا سَمِعُوا۟ ٱلذِّكْرَ - “when they heard the Reminder.” Their hostile stares intensified when the Quran was recited. The mention of this looking is significant because people can insult and attack without saying a single word, simply through their facial expressions and the way they direct their gaze. A hateful stare, a contemptuous glance, eyes rolled in mockery - these are crimes of the heart that manifest through the eyes before the tongue ever speaks.

وَيَقُولُونَ إِنَّهُۥ لَمَجْنُونٌ - “and they say, ‘Indeed, he is surely insane.’” After committing the crime of the hateful stare, they voice their accusation: possessed, mad, his intellect overshadowed by jinn. The word مَجْنُونٌ suggests someone whose intellect has been covered over, whose reasoning has been compromised by unseen forces.

Ayah 52

وَمَا هُوَ إِلَّا ذِكْرٌ لِّلْعَـٰلَمِينَ

The surah concludes with a definitive statement: وَمَا هُوَ إِلَّا ذِكْرٌ لِّلْعَـٰلَمِينَ - “And it is not except a reminder to the worlds.”

This final ayah brings beautiful closure to the entire surah. Notice that Allah ﷻ consoled His Messenger ﷺ about the accusation of insanity at the very beginning of the surah, providing proof and reassurance that he was not mad at all. Now at the end, when the disbelievers repeat their accusation, it no longer carries the same sting. The Prophet ﷺ has already been affirmed and reassured by Allah ﷻ Himself, so their words have lost their power to wound.

The structure of the surah also reflects the two inseparable components of guidance: the messenger and the message. The surah began by highlighting the messenger himself - his character, his integrity, his sanity. It now ends by highlighting the message itself - the Quran as a reminder for all humanity. These two elements cannot be separated. The one delivering the message possesses remarkable character, and the message itself carries undeniable truth and wisdom.

How can either be dismissed as lies or the product of madness? The surah that began with defending the Prophet’s ﷺ sanity concludes by affirming the universal relevance and divine origin of the message he carries. Both the messenger and the message stand vindicated, while the accusations of the disbelievers are exposed as baseless and hollow.

Note: no commentary published yet for ayah(s) 4–10, 17–37, 44–50, 52.

Ayah 1–2 · linked video: 01. Al-Qalam (Ayah 1-2)

Root Word Analysis

Ayah 1

نٓ (noon)

This is a disjointed letter (harf muqatta’at) at the beginning of the surah. The letter noon stands alone without a clear lexical meaning in this context. These mysterious letters at the beginning of certain surahs have been subject to various interpretations by scholars, with some suggesting they represent names of Allah, others believing they are divine secrets known only to Allah, and yet others proposing they serve as attention-grabbing devices for the listeners.

الْقَلَمِ (al-qalam) [ق-ل-م]

The root ق-ل-م refers to the pen, the instrument of writing. The definite article ‘al’ (the) gives it emphasis and universality - not just any pen, but THE pen as a concept and tool. The qalam represents the means by which knowledge is recorded, preserved and transmitted across generations. This is the primary tool mentioned in the oath.

يَسْطُرُونَ (yasturuna) [س-ط-ر]

The root س-ط-ر means to write or to record in lines. The verb form is in the present/future tense (mudari’), indicating continuous, ongoing action. The use of the plural form (they write) without a clear antecedent is deliberate - it leaves the subject open to interpretation. It could refer to angels recording deeds, scribes writing revelation, or future generations of scholars producing works of knowledge. The استمرار (continuity) in the verb form suggests both past and future writing - what has been written and what will continue to be written.

Ayah 2

مَا (ma)

This is a negative particle meaning ‘not’ - it completely negates what follows.

أَنتَ (anta)

The second person singular pronoun ‘you’ - directly addressing the Prophet Muhammad صلى الله عليه وسلم.

بِنِعْمَةِ (bi ni’mati) [ن-ع-م]

The root ن-ع-م means blessing, favor, grace. The word نِعْمَة (ni’mah) refers to a special favor or blessing. The preposition بِ (bi) means ‘by’ or ‘through’ - indicating the means or cause. This phrase ‘by the blessing of your Lord’ serves as an emphatic insertion that strengthens the negation, essentially saying ‘by your Lord’s special favor upon you.’

رَبِّكَ (rabbika) [ر-ب-ب]

The root ر-ب-ب means to nurture, sustain, raise, and care for. رَبّ (Rabb) is Lord, Master, Sustainer. The possessive suffix كَ (your) creates an intimate, personal relationship - ‘YOUR Lord’ - emphasizing the special connection between the Prophet صلى الله عليه وسلم and Allah جل جلاله.

بِمَجْنُونٍ (bi majnun) [ج-ن-ن]

The root ج-ن-ن means to be hidden, covered, or concealed - from which comes جِنّ (jinn, hidden beings) and جُنُون (junun, madness/insanity). A مَجْنُون is someone whose intellect is hidden or covered - insane, mad, possessed. The preposition بِ here is زَائِدَة (additional) for emphasis. The word مَجْنُون is in the indefinite form (without al-) which in a negative context means ‘not at all’ - you are not insane in any way whatsoever.

Grammatical & Rhetorical Nuances

Ayah 1

The surah opens with قَسَم (oath/swearing) - a powerful rhetorical device where Allah جل جلاله swears by His creation. The structure “نٓ وَالْقَلَمِ وَمَا يَسْطُرُونَ” uses the oath particle و (wa) before القلم, making it the primary object of the oath.

The deliberate ambiguity of the pronoun in يَسْطُرُونَ (they write) is a rhetorical technique called إِبْهَام (ibham - ambiguity). By not specifying who ‘they’ are, Allah جل جلاله opens multiple layers of meaning simultaneously - angels, scribes, scholars, all are included in this vast canvas of ‘writing.’

The use of the مُضَارِع (present/future tense) in يَسْطُرُونَ rather than past tense is significant. It indicates استمرار (continuity) - the writing that was happening, is happening, and will continue to happen. This grammatical choice expands the timeframe of the oath from the immediate present to encompass all of history.

The مَا in “وَمَا يَسْطُرُونَ” is a mawsul (relative pronoun) meaning ‘that which’ or ‘whatever’ - it creates a comprehensive, all-encompassing reference to everything that is written by the pen.

Ayah 2

The جُمْلَة إِسْمِيَّة (nominal sentence) structure “مَا أَنتَ بِنِعْمَةِ رَبِّكَ بِمَجْنُونٍ” creates emphasis. The placement of the subject أَنتَ (you) immediately after the negative particle مَا intensifies the negation.

The insertion of “بِنِعْمَةِ رَبِّكَ” (by the blessing of your Lord) between the subject and predicate is called اعْتِرَاض (i’tirad - parenthetical insertion). This grammatical interruption serves multiple rhetorical purposes: it breaks up the sentence for emphasis, it provides the reason/cause for the negation, and it offers consolation to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وسلم by reminding him of Allah’s favor.

The double use of the preposition بِ (bi) - once in “بِنِعْمَةِ” and again in “بِمَجْنُونٍ” - creates a rhetorical balance. The first بِ is causative (by means of), while the second is زَائِدَة (additional for emphasis in negation).

The indefinite form مَجْنُونٍ (without the definite article) in a negative context creates what’s called نَفْي الجِنْس (negation of the entire category) - ‘not insane in any way, shape, or form.’ This is stronger than saying “you are not THE insane one” - it completely removes any possibility of insanity.

The إِضَافَة (possessive construction) in “نِعْمَةِ رَبِّكَ” followed by another إِضَافَة with the possessive pronoun creates layers of intimacy and specificity - ‘the blessing of YOUR Lord’ - emphasizing the special, personal relationship between the Prophet صلى الله عليه وسلم and Allah جل جلاله.

Historical & Biblical Context

Ayah 1

The pen holds profound significance in Judeo-Christian scripture and tradition. According to the lecture, the Torah and Gospel were transmitted primarily through writing, not memorization like the Quran. The Jewish tradition employed panels of scribes (soferim) who carefully documented and preserved revelation. This scribal tradition was central to how previous scriptures were preserved across generations.

The mention of the pen as the first thing sworn by after the letter noon connects to the very first revelation to Prophet Muhammad صلى الله عليه وسلم: “اقْرَأْ بِاسْمِ رَبِّكَ الَّذِي خَلَقَ” (Read in the name of your Lord who created). That first revelation continues with “عَلَّمَ بِالْقَلَمِ” (taught by the pen) - establishing the pen as the divine instrument of knowledge transmission from the very beginning of the prophetic mission.

The reference to ‘what they write’ encompasses the centuries of Jewish and Christian scholarship that existed at the time of revelation. The lecture emphasizes that the Quran takes entire chapters of Jewish history and condenses them into single verses or phrases. When Allah جل جلاله swears by the pen and what is written, He is validating that the previous scriptures - despite their alterations - contain enough truth to serve as witnesses to the authenticity of Muhammad’s صلى الله عليه وسلم message.

The lecture discusses how Musa (AS) appears more frequently in the Quran than any other prophet, and how four out of five books of the Torah deal with his era. When these verses were revealed, the primary audience included Jews who had extensive knowledge of their scriptures. The Quran’s selective retelling of these stories - mentioning some details while omitting others - was deliberate communication with an audience that already had a framework of understanding.

Ayah 2

The accusation of insanity (junun) against prophets has biblical precedent. When Musa (AS) received revelation at the mountain, according to Jewish tradition, he was alone herding sheep - though the Quran clarifies his family was with him when he saw the fire. The isolation and dramatic nature of prophetic experiences often led to accusations of madness from those who witnessed them.

Some scholars hold that these opening seven verses of Surah Al-Qalam may have been the second revelation after Surah Al-’Alaq. The Prophet صلى الله عليه وسلم, after the traumatic experience of first revelation in the cave of Hira, returned home shaken, saying “Cover me, cover me!” (zammilooni). Some close to him wondered if he had lost his mind from the experience. This verse would then be Allah’s immediate reassurance to His messenger.

The lecture emphasizes a crucial historical reality often forgotten: the Prophet صلى الله عليه وسلم himself had to have faith in his own prophethood. He had to believe in himself as a messenger - “That’s not easy to believe if you’re the only one saying it.” This human dimension of prophetic experience is important to understand the psychological weight of receiving this divine negation of insanity accusations.

The broader context involves accusations from the Quraysh that Muhammad صلى الله عليه وسلم was possessed by jinn, was a madman, or was under some form of supernatural influence that explained his claims. These accusations are addressed and refuted throughout the Meccan period of revelation.

Coherence

Ayah 1

Surah Al-Qalam is the last surah that has its own dedicated juz’ (Juz’ 29), marking it as significant in the Quran’s structure. The oath structure at the beginning creates thematic coherence with other Meccan surahs that use similar openings - oaths by elements of creation to validate the message.

The mention of the pen connects directly to the first revelation (Surah Al-’Alaq 96:1-5), creating a thematic bridge across the Quran. The emphasis on the pen and writing establishes a foundational theme about knowledge, literacy, and the preservation of truth that runs throughout the Quran.

The ambiguous pronoun ‘they’ in “what they write” creates multiple layers of connection: - Angels writing deeds: connects to themes of accountability and the Day of Judgment found throughout the Quran - Angels recording revelation: connects to themes about the preservation and sanctity of the Quran - Scribes of previous scriptures: connects to themes about the People of the Book and previous revelations - Future scholars: connects to themes about the Ummah’s role in preserving and transmitting knowledge

This single verse thus weaves together past, present and future into a unified testimony.

Ayah 2

This verse functions as the جَوَاب القَسَم (answer to the oath) - the point being proven by the oath of the previous verse. The structure “I swear by X, verily Y” is a common Quranic pattern where the oath (X) proves the statement (Y).

The connection is logical: the pen and what is written serve as evidence that the Prophet صلى الله عليه وسلم is not insane. How? Because: 1. Previous scriptures contain prophecies and descriptions that validate his message 2. The literary miracle of what he himself is producing (the Quran) proves sanity 3. The future scholarship and knowledge systems that will emerge from his message will prove his sanity

The phrase “by the blessing of your Lord” creates thematic coherence with the nurturing, caring aspect of Allah’s relationship with His prophets found throughout the Quran. The term رَبّ (Rabb - Lord/Nurturer) appears consistently when Allah جل جلاله addresses His messengers in moments of difficulty or challenge.

The direct address أَنتَ (you) to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وسلم in second person is relatively rare in the Quran compared to third person references. This direct, intimate address creates a moment of personal reassurance within the broader message to humanity.

These opening verses set the tone for the entire surah, which will continue to address opposition to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وسلم and validate his message against accusations. The surah builds from this foundational statement of sanity to discuss the character of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وسلم and contrast it with his opponents.

Psychological, Political & Social

Ayah 1

The lecture emphasizes the experiential dimension of Quran engagement - that true understanding comes not just through intellectual study but through the spiritual experience of hearing it recited in salah. This speaks to a psychological reality: knowledge without spiritual connection can actually diminish faith rather than strengthen it. The rabbi’s testimony about studying Jewish law for eight years only to realize he didn’t know why he was learning it reflects a danger in purely intellectual religious engagement divorced from faith and practice.

The emphasis on the pen and writing reflects a social revolution. In a largely oral society (7th century Arabia), the Quran’s emphasis on literacy, writing and the pen represented a dramatic shift in social values. Knowledge preservation through writing would transform the Muslim community’s approach to learning and scholarship.

The ambiguity in ‘what they write’ creates inclusive meaning - it’s not restricted to one group or one time. This reflects a psychological wisdom: by leaving it open, every generation of Muslims can find themselves included in this divine oath. Future scholars reading this verse understand that their work of writing and scholarship is validated by Allah جل جلاله Himself.

Ayah 2

The accusation of insanity represents one of the most devastating social attacks in any community - questioning someone’s mental stability undermines their credibility completely. For the Prophet صلى الله عليه وسلم, who was known as Al-Amin (the trustworthy) before prophethood, this accusation was particularly painful.

The psychological dimension discussed in the lecture is profound: “The Prophet صلى الله عليه وسلم wasn’t even sure… He had to believe too. We think we have to have faith in him. He had to have faith in himself.” This humanizes the prophetic experience. The psychological burden of claiming prophethood, of being the sole voice making this claim initially, required immense courage and faith. Allah’s reassurance “you are not insane at all” addresses this internal psychological struggle, not just external accusations.

The phrase “by the blessing of your Lord” serves as psychological grounding - reminding the Prophet صلى الله عليه وسلم that his experience is a ni’mah (blessing), not a curse or affliction. This reframing is psychologically powerful: what others call madness, Allah calls blessing.

Socially, this verse establishes a pattern that would become crucial for the Muslim community: when the world questions your sanity for holding firm to truth, remember that truth validated by divine knowledge is true sanity. This would sustain Muslims through persecution in Mecca and throughout Islamic history.

The lecture’s discussion about interfaith dialogue and meeting with the rabbi illustrates contemporary application of these themes. The courage required to engage across deep divides, to face social disapproval from one’s own community (the rabbi losing donors), parallels the courage required of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وسلم to persist despite accusations of insanity. It also reflects the Quranic principle embedded in these verses - that previous scriptures (‘what they write’) can serve as points of dialogue and understanding, not just conflict.

Quranic Wisdom

Ayah 1

The divine oath by the pen establishes knowledge, literacy and documentation as sacred values in Islam. The pen is not merely a tool - it is worthy of divine oath, elevated to the status where Allah جل جلاله swears by it. This teaches us that the pursuit of knowledge, the act of writing, and the preservation of wisdom are among the highest human endeavors.

The continuity implied in يَسْطُرُونَ (they write) - past, present and future - teaches that we are part of a chain of knowledge transmission. Every scholar, every student who writes notes, every person who documents understanding is participating in something Allah جل جلاله has sworn by. This should give us reverence for the act of learning and teaching.

The ambiguity of ‘they’ teaches us that revelation speaks on multiple levels simultaneously. The Quran is not one-dimensional; it addresses different audiences and different time periods with the same words. This multidimensionality is part of its miracle and its timelessness.

The lecture’s emphasis on experiencing Quran in salah rather than just studying it intellectually teaches a crucial lesson: knowledge must be connected to practice, to worship, to lived experience. The goal of studying tafsir is not accumulation of information but transformation of our prayer experience, our connection with Allah جل جلاله.

Ayah 2

The emphatic negation “you are not at all insane” teaches that when you stand for truth, accusations will come - including attacks on your very sanity and credibility. The world will question whether you’re mentally sound for holding to principles others have abandoned. Allah’s response is not to ignore these accusations but to directly, emphatically negate them.

The insertion “by the blessing of your Lord” teaches that divine favor is the ultimate validation. Human opinion, social acceptance, popularity - none of these matter when you have the ni’mah (blessing) of your Lord. This reframing is essential: what looks like difficulty or persecution from outside is actually a sign of divine favor from the inside.

The possessive “your Lord” teaches the intimate, personal nature of our relationship with Allah جل جلاله. He is not just ‘the Lord’ in abstract - He is YOUR Lord, personally invested in you, personally defending you, personally blessing you. This personal relationship is what gives courage in the face of opposition.

The deeper wisdom is about prophetic psychology: even prophets need reassurance. Even Prophet Muhammad صلى الله عليه وسلم needed to be told explicitly that he was not insane. This teaches us that doubt, fear and uncertainty are part of the human experience - even for the best of humans. Faith is not the absence of doubt; it’s choosing truth despite doubt. The Prophet صلى الله عليه وسلم “had to have faith in himself” - this struggle with self-belief makes his ultimate steadfastness even more remarkable and more relatable.

The lecture’s discussion about engaging with people of other faiths despite social pressure reflects this same wisdom. When you pursue truth and knowledge, when you engage in dialogue that others call dangerous or wrong, you need the same conviction these verses gave the Prophet صلى الله عليه وسلم: that you are not crazy for seeking understanding, that this is actually a path of blessing (ni’mah), and that your Lord is with you in this journey.

Ultimately, these verses teach that truth is its own defense. The pen, the written word, knowledge itself will vindicate the message. You don’t need to defend yourself desperately against false accusations - time, knowledge and the written record will speak for themselves. This gives believers patience and confidence in the face of opposition.

Ayah 2–3 · linked video: 03. Al-Qalam (Ayah 2-3)

Root Word Analysis

Ayah 2

مَا (ma) [negation particle]

While technically a particle, this negation marker is significant here. The ma form is used for emphatic refutation, stronger than the standard lasta construction. It establishes the intensity of Allah’s defense of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وسلم against accusations of insanity.

نِعْمَة (ni’mah) [ن-ع-م]

The root ن-ع-م relates to softness, gentleness, comfort and luxury. The inner sole of an ostrich’s foot is extra soft (called ni’mah). Trees with soft leaves are described as na’ima. Soft, luxurious cloth is na’uma. When something becomes gentle or soft, that quality is expressed through this root. Cattle move softly, so they’re called an’am. A life of comfort and wealth is hayat an-na’im. Women living cushioned lives, having everything taken care of, are described with this root—even spoiled women who cannot handle hardship. Winds that felt particularly pleasant were called nu’ama. The grammatical form ni’mah (favor/blessing) carries all these connotations of softness, ease, comfort and luxury—precisely what the Prophet صلى الله عليه وسلم was NOT experiencing externally while being insulted, humiliated and physically threatened. This creates a powerful contrast: the ni’mah is the Quran itself, the direct communication with Allah that provides spiritual comfort in the midst of worldly hardship.

رَبّ (rabb) [ر-ب-ب]

The root ر-ب-ب means master, nurturer, sustainer, the one who raises and takes care of. The possessive form rabbika (your master) emphasizes the personal relationship between Allah and His Prophet صلى الله عليه وسلم. This isn’t just “God” in an abstract sense—it’s “your Master” who will protect you against these allegations.

مَجْنُون (majnun) [ج-ن-ن]

The root ج-ن-ن fundamentally means “covered” or “hidden.” Jannah (garden) is called so because it’s covered in greenery. Junnah (shield/armor) covers the body. Al-janin (fetus) is covered by the womb. Al-janan (grave) covers the body with dirt. The janajin (ribs) cover the heart. Jinn are called jinn because our eyes are veiled from seeing them—they’re hidden. Majnun has two core meanings stemming from this root: (1) possessed by jinn—a jinn has covered over/taken over the person, and (2) insane—the mind has been overshadowed or covered, mental faculties lost. In ancient understanding, these two concepts were often interchangeable. The double emphasis in the ayah (ma + bi) strongly refutes both meanings.

Ayah 3

أَجْر (ajr) [أ-ج-ر]

The root أ-ج-ر relates to physically taxing, backbreaking labor. Ajur refers to barbecue pits dug deep into the ground, requiring significant physical effort. Workers who use axes to cut rocks and minerals from mountains (mine workers) are called ujara. The person doing such labor is the ajir, and the compensation for this exhausting work is the ajr. This word is not used lightly—it’s specifically for rewards that have been truly earned through difficult effort. The Prophet صلى الله عليه وسلم is being told his mission is recognized as heavy, laborious work, and he will be compensated accordingly.

غَيْر (ghayr) [غ-ي-ر]

The root غ-ي-ر means “other than” or “without.” When combined with mamnun, it creates the meaning “without discontinuation” or “never-ending.”

مَمْنُون (mamnun) [م-ن-ن]

The root م-ن-ن has multiple rich layers of meaning. First, mann refers to a rope used to drain a well by pulling so much water that nothing remains—exhaustion, depletion. A journey that exhausts someone completely is described with this root. Mann also means strength or power, so manin describes someone who has become weakened over time. Second, manna means to remind someone of a favor you did for them, seeking to cash in credit or make them feel obligated. This can be done to gain something in return or simply to assert superiority. Third, the pattern ghayr mamnun together means infinite, unending, never weakening. The combination of ajr (earned compensation) with ghayr mamnun creates a powerful paradox: labor is finite, so compensation should be finite, yet the Prophet’s reward is infinite—it starts as ajr but becomes something entirely beyond normal compensation.

خُلُق (khuluq) [خ-ل-ق]

The root خ-ل-ق relates to character, moral nature, disposition and innate qualities. The word khuluq specifically refers to character and ethical behavior, distinct from khalq (physical creation). The construction wa innaka la’ala khuluqin ’adhim creates powerful imagery: character is presented as a mountain, and the Prophet صلى الله عليه وسلم is described as being on top of that mountain—having conquered and surpassed it.

عَظِيم (’adhim) [ع-ظ-م]

The root ع-ظ-م means great, magnificent, tremendous. ’Adhim indicates something of immense magnitude and importance. The khuluq is not just good—it’s ’adhim, tremendous in its scope and perfection.

Grammatical & Rhetorical Nuances

Ayah 2

The ayah employs double emphasis through two distinct grammatical structures. First, the negation particle ma is inherently stronger than the standard lasta construction—ma is used for emphatic refutation. Second, the ba’ in bi-majnun is called ba’ az-za’idah (the extra ba’), adding another layer of emphasis. Together, these create the meaning “you are not insane at all” rather than a simple “you are not insane.” This emphatic structure reflects the intensity needed in the moment—Allah is not making a casual statement but delivering a forceful rebuttal.

The phrase bi-ni’mati rabbika is strategically placed in the middle of the denial, creating a rhetorical shift. It transforms the entire framework of the accusation: what they call proof of insanity (the Quran) is reframed as proof of divine favor. The preposition bi carries dual meaning: “because of” (causal) and “by means of” (instrumental). Because Allah has favored him, insanity cannot touch him. By means of Allah’s favor (the Quran and angelic protection), he is shielded from jinn possession.

The possessive rabbika (your master) creates intimacy and personal connection rather than using a general divine name. This grammatical choice emphasizes the special relationship and protection.

Ayah 3

The construction wa inna laka begins with the emphatic particle inna, which, when combined with the lam of emphasis (la-ajran), creates a strong assertion: “you truly do have” or “you exclusively have.” This grammatical emphasis mirrors the emphasis in the previous ayah.

The word ajr appears in its indefinite form (nakira), which in Arabic creates a sense of vastness and indefinability. Allah could have said “wa inna al-ajra laka” (the specific reward), but by using the indefinite form, the meaning becomes “a reward of such magnitude and variety that it cannot be limited or fully described.”

The phrase ghayr mamnun employs a negative particle (ghayr) to describe the reward through what it is NOT rather than what it IS. This rhetorical device (describing through negation) emphasizes the unprecedented nature of this compensation—it defies normal categories of reward.
